I'd also like to mention a thing about the ups and downs of preemptive surveys. So, um, serving extensively before doing research has a bunch of good sides. So it prevents you from duplicating work. So somebody else might have done a very similar thing.
It also increases your toolbox method. So, you know, if it's a problem that a lot of people have worked on before, then, you know, it helps give you ideas and methods that you could be using.

However, in a way, it also kind of constrains your thinking. So, um, if you like one, once you have built up a very extensive survey of like ways to do things, you tend to like move away from there. It's called how to build an economics model in your spare time. It's about it's from a Nobel Prize winner in economics, but he's talking about how when he tries to come up with new and like important ideas, he doesn't look at economics journals. He looks at the newspaper and tries to, you know, like.
Look at problems that people are talking about in the newspaper and think about whether there's an economic solution to them.

And so if we think about the analog of how we can do this in natural language processing, you know, maybe you don't necessarily right away want to do a really extensive survey. First, you might just think about like what's bothering you.
